notes.note: The opening of a letter from C. Trebonius (governor of Asia, one of Caesar's assassins) to Cicero. The S. is salutem (dicit), "sends greetings".

notes.anchor: If you are well, it is well.
notes.note: Renders the formulaic Latin opener S. v. b. (si vales, bene), a conventional epistolary courtesy inquiring after the recipient's health.

notes.anchor: your son devoted to the best studies
notes.note: Cicero's son Marcus, then about eighteen, was studying philosophy and rhetoric at Athens, the standard finishing education for young Roman aristocrats.
The Peripatetic philosopher under whom the younger Marcus studied at Athens; Cicero held him in high esteem and dedicated the De Officiis to his son partly on the strength of this teaching.
Trebonius glosses his Latin with the Greek euthyrrhemonesteros ("rather plain-spoken"), apologising for the satirical bluntness of the verses he encloses.

Gaius Lucilius (2nd c. BC), founder of Roman verse satire, was proverbial for the freedom (libertas) with which he attacked named contemporaries; Trebonius invokes him as a precedent for his own invective.

The Latin de interitu Caesaris refers to a projected literary treatment of the dictator's assassination on 15 March 44 BC; Trebonius, himself a conspirator, asks to be given his share of the credit in any such account.
